agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business value proposition.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management — intersects with Compliance, Customer Reporting, Dispute Resolution, and Financial Audit bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: pickup, transit, proof of delivery, timestamps, signatures, GPS traces, chain-of-custody events. This is a legal document, not just operational data."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a consignment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, timestamps. Required for dispute resolution and insurance claims."},{"term":"Data Retention Period","definition":"The legally and contractually mandated minimum duration that records must be preserved before they may be deleted."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT override legal retention obligations under Art. 17(3)(b)."},{"term":"Statute of Limitations","definition":"The legal window during which claims can be brought. In the UK this is typically 6 years (Limitation Act 1980); in many EU jurisdictions 3-10 years."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The documented sequence of possession of a consignment from sender to recipient. Critical for liability determination."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit requirements — a core part of the platform's value proposition to enterprise clients."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for the longer of: (a) the applicable statute of limitations for contractual and tort claims, or (b) the contractually agreed retention period with each customer.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting records before the statute of limitations expires destroys evidence needed for legal defense. This is not a policy preference — it is a legal survival requirement. If a customer sues for a lost/damaged shipment in year 3 and the records were deleted at day 90, the platform cannot defend itself.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record is deleted while any applicable statute of limitations or contractual retention obligation remains in effect."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery artifacts (signatures, photos, GPS data) must be retained at least as long as the delivery record they belong to.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"POD is the primary evidence in delivery disputes. A delivery record without its POD is legally useless — the two must share the same retention lifecycle.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery record with retention date T, all associated POD artifacts have retention date >= T."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customers must have access to delivery data for the full duration specified in their service agreement or SLA.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that B2B customers depend on for their own compliance reporting is a breach of contract and destroys the platform's enterprise value proposition.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record accessible to a B2B customer is deleted before the retention period in that customer's service agreement expires."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Tax and customs-related delivery records in the EU/UK must be retained for the minimum period required by the applicable tax authority (typically 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit historical periods. Destroying records within the audit window is a regulatory violation that can result in fines and presumption of liability.","testable_assertion":"Delivery records linked to cross-border shipments or VAT-relevant transactions are retained for the jurisdiction's required tax retention period."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR does not require deletion of delivery records within 90 days. Art. 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts data retained for compliance with a legal obligation, and Art. 17(3)(e) exempts data needed for legal claims.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal interpretation that should be confirmed by legal counsel, but it is well-established in practice. GDPR requires data minimization, not premature destruction of legally required records.","testable_assertion":"Data retention policy documentation cites specific legal bases for each retention period under GDPR Art. 6(1)(c) or Art. 6(1)(f)."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2C customer files a claim for a damaged delivery 4 months after delivery. Under the 90-day policy, the delivery record and POD have been deleted.","expected_behavior":"The platform cannot defend against the claim, cannot verify what was delivered, and is presumed liable. This is a direct financial loss."},{"scenario":"A B2B logistics customer needs delivery records from 6 months ago for a tax audit by their national authority.","expected_behavior":"Under the 90-day policy, the data is gone. The B2B customer fails their audit, blames the platform, terminates their contract, and potentially sues for damages."},{"scenario":"HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the platform's cross-border delivery activity for the previous 3 years.","expected_behavior":"Under the 90-day policy, 97% of the auditable records are destroyed. The platform faces penalties, estimated assessments, and potential fraud allegations."},{"scenario":"An insurance claim for lost goods is filed 8 months after the delivery date.","expected_behavior":"No chain-of-custody records exist. The platform cannot prove it delivered the goods. Insurance claim defaults against the platform."},{"scenario":"A GDPR data subject access request arrives for a delivery made 60 days ago, and separately for one made 120 days ago.","expected_behavior":"The 60-day record exists and can be provided. The 120-day record has been deleted, so the platform responds that no data is held — but if the record was legally required to be retained longer, the premature deletion itself may be a compliance violation."},{"scenario":"A class-action claim is brought against the platform for systematic delivery failures over a 2-year period.","expected_behavior":"Under the 90-day policy, the platform has no historical records to analyze patterns, defend itself, or even quantify exposure."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Storage cost savings are trivial compared to exposure. Cloud storage for structured delivery records is measured in thousands of dollars; a single undefended lawsuit or tax penalty is measured in hundreds of thousands to millions. (2) B2B customer churn: enterprise logistics customers choose platforms partly for data reliability and compliance support. Losing access to historical data is a contract-breaking event. (3) Insurance premiums will increase if the platform cannot provide evidence for claims defense. (4) Tax penalties for destroyed records can include estimated assessments where the authority assumes maximum liability.","operational":"SIGNIFICANT NEGATIVE. (1) Customer support loses ability to investigate complaints older than 90 days. (2) Dispute resolution becomes impossible — the platform defaults to customer-favorable outcomes, increasing costs. (3) Analytics and business intelligence lose historical trend data. (4) Fraud detection patterns that rely on historical comparison are degraded. If storage cost is the real concern, the correct operational approach is tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) — not deletion.","user":"SEVERE NEGATIVE for both segments. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries, file delayed claims, or get support for issues discovered after 90 days. B2B customers lose a core platform capability they likely chose the platform for — reliable long-term delivery data for compliance reporting. Trust erosion is difficult to reverse.","risk":"CRITICAL. (1) Legal/regulatory risk: destruction of records within statute of limitations and tax audit windows. (2) Contractual risk: breach of SLAs with B2B customers that specify data availability periods. (3) Litigation risk: inability to defend against delivery claims, resulting in default liability. (4) Regulatory risk: potential GDPR violation if deletion removes data the platform was legally obligated to retain. (5) Reputational risk: being known as the platform that deletes your data after 90 days is a competitive disadvantage in the enterprise logistics market.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — lose access to delivery data needed for their own compliance, tax, and audit reporting. Likely contract breach. High churn risk."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — lose ability to reference deliveries, file claims, or get support for issues older than 90 days."},{"group":"Legal & Compliance Team","impact":"CRITICAL — the platform loses its ability to defend against claims and comply with tax authority audits. The legal team will inherit undefendable cases."},{"group":"Finance / Tax","impact":"CRITICAL — cross-border VAT records and customs documentation destroyed within tax audit windows. Direct regulatory exposure."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"HIGH — cannot investigate or resolve any issue older than 90 days. Increased unresolved complaints and compensation payouts."},{"group":"Insurance Partners","impact":"HIGH — claims cannot be substantiated with delivery evidence. Premium increases or coverage denial."},{"group":"Product & Sales","impact":"HIGH — enterprise sales proposition weakened. Historical delivery analytics and reporting features gutted."},{"group":"Engineering / Infrastructure","impact":"LOW POSITIVE — reduced storage footprint, simpler data management. This is the only stakeholder group that benefits."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because this proposal has critical legal and regulatory dimensions that require review by qualified legal counsel in each operating jurisdiction. The general direction of my analysis (90 days is far too short) is high-confidence based on well-known EU/UK legal requirements, but the exact minimum retention periods vary by jurisdiction and by the specific nature of the delivery (domestic vs. cross-border, value of goods, hazardous materials, etc.). The framing as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect and suggests the proposing team may not have consulted legal or commercial stakeholders.","high_confidence_areas":["90 days is below the statute of limitations in all EU/UK jurisdictions","B2B customers will be materially impacted","Tax authorities require retention well beyond 90 days","This is NOT merely an infrastructure optimization"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact minimum retention period per jurisdiction (requires legal counsel)","Specific contractual obligations in existing B2B agreements","Whether the platform has cross-border or customs-relevant deliveries that trigger additional retention requirements","Current storage cost figures to quantify the actual savings being pursued"],"assumptions":["The platform handles physical delivery of goods, not purely digital services","B2B customer contracts include data availability commitments","The platform operates cross-border within EU/UK, triggering VAT and customs record requirements","No legal counsel has reviewed or approved the 90-day proposal"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but delivery record retention IS a business rule — it directly affects dispute resolution, compliance reporting, legal defensibility, and contractual obligations. Auto-deleting records after 90 days fundamentally changes the platform's legal and commercial posture. This framing must be corrected before any further work proceeds.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal & Compliance","B2B Customers","Finance / Tax","Customer Support","Insurance Partners"],"options":["A) Reject the 90-day deletion proposal entirely and retain the 7-year policy","B) Implement tiered storage (hot → warm → cold/archive) to reduce costs while preserving all data for the legally required period","C) Conduct a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal review to determine the true minimum retention periods, then implement per-jurisdiction retention rules with tiered storage"],"recommendation":"Option C is the correct long-term approach, with Option B as an immediate cost-reduction measure. Option A (status quo) is the safe default until legal review is complete. Under NO circumstances should Option 'auto-delete at 90 days' proceed."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The exact minimum legal retention periods for delivery records in each EU/UK jurisdiction the platform operates in have not been established by qualified legal counsel. General knowledge suggests 3-10 years depending on jurisdiction and record type, but this must be confirmed before any retention policy change.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal & Compliance","Engineering"],"options":["Engage legal counsel to produce a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention schedule","Use the current 7-year policy as a safe default until legal review completes"],"recommendation":"Engage legal counsel immediately. Do not change retention periods without a signed-off legal opinion for each operating jurisdiction."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion destroys delivery records within the statute of limitations period in ALL EU/UK jurisdictions, making the platform unable to defend against contractual and tort claims.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Proof of Delivery artifacts would be destroyed at 90 days, eliminating the platform's ability to prove deliveries occurred for any dispute filed after that window.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"B2B customers who depend on delivery data for compliance reporting would lose access to records, constituting a likely breach of service agreements.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Tax and customs records for cross-border EU/UK deliveries would be destroyed years before the required retention period expires, creating direct regulatory exposure.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"The proposal is incorrectly framed as 'no business logic changes' — data retention for legally significant records IS business logic, and this change fundamentally alters the platform's compliance and legal defensibility posture.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-3, BR-4"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business value proposition.","bounded_context":
